title: Apache Airflow: Intermediate Track
description: Master intermediate Airflow patterns: TaskFlow API, custom operators and hooks, dynamic task mapping, Datasets and event-driven scheduling, cross-DAG orchestration, testing strategies, CI/CD deployment, performance tuning, security and RBAC, and a production capstone.

chapters.title: TaskFlow API - The Modern Interface
chapters.description: Replace verbose operator boilerplate with the @task decorator pattern, understand how TaskFlow manages XCom automatically, and convert existing DAGs to the modern interface.

blocks.content: Every DAG you built in the beginner track used PythonOperator to wrap your task functions. It worked, but it came with a tax: manual XCom push and pull, explicit task_instance references, **context unpacking, and >> dependency declarations even when the data flow was obvious. TaskFlow eliminates all of that tax.
The Boilerplate Problem

Consider a simple three-step pipeline for NordGrid's meter readings: extract the file path from SFTP, validate the row count, and load to Silver. In the classic pattern, each step requires a PythonOperator, each inter-task data transfer requires explicit XCom push/pull calls, and the dependency chain requires a separate >> line at the bottom.

# Classic pattern - NordGrid meter pipeline
from airflow import DAG
from airflow.operators.python import PythonOperator
from datetime import datetime

def extract_meters(**context):
    file_path = f"s3://nordgrid-raw/meters/{context['ds_nodash']}.csv"
    row_count = 1_200_000  # simulated
    context['ti'].xcom_push(key='file_path', value=file_path)
    context['ti'].xcom_push(key='row_count', value=row_count)

def validate_meters(**context):
    file_path = context['ti'].xcom_pull(task_ids='extract', key='file_path')
    row_count = context['ti'].xcom_pull(task_ids='extract', key='row_count')
    if row_count < 100_000:
        raise ValueError(f"Row count {row_count} below threshold for {file_path}")
    context['ti'].xcom_push(key='validated_path', value=file_path)

def load_to_silver(**context):
    path = context['ti'].xcom_pull(task_ids='validate', key='validated_path')
    print(f"Loading {path} to silver layer")

with DAG('classic_meter_pipeline', start_date=datetime(2026, 1, 1),
         schedule='@daily', catchup=False) as dag:

    extract = PythonOperator(task_id='extract', python_callable=extract_meters)
    validate = PythonOperator(task_id='validate', python_callable=validate_meters)
    load = PythonOperator(task_id='load', python_callable=load_to_silver)

    extract >> validate >> load

Count the boilerplate: six xcom_push/pull calls, three context dictionary unpacks, three PythonOperator instantiations, and a dependency chain that duplicates what the data flow already implies. For three tasks this is manageable. For a Silver layer pipeline with twelve tasks, it becomes a maintenance burden.
The TaskFlow Solution

TaskFlow replaces all of that with decorated Python functions. You return values instead of pushing XCom. You pass arguments instead of pulling XCom. Airflow infers dependencies from the function call graph. The same pipeline becomes:

blocks.title: Key insight
blocks.content: Zero XCom calls. Zero PythonOperator instantiations. Zero >> declarations. The dependency graph is inferred entirely from the Python function call chain. What you return flows to whoever calls for it.
What Changed and What Stayed

left.label: Classic Pattern
points: DAG and tasks are separate objects

blocks.content: Everything under the hood is identical. The same Airflow scheduler parses the DAG. The same executor runs the tasks. The same XCom backend stores inter-task data. TaskFlow is syntactic sugar - but it is the kind of sugar that eliminates entire categories of bugs.

The @task decorator is the foundation of TaskFlow. It transforms a regular Python function into an Airflow task. Understanding exactly what happens when you apply it - at parse time and at execution time - is the key to using it correctly.
Anatomy of a TaskFlow Function

from airflow.decorators import task

@task(
    task_id='validate_readings',       # optional: defaults to function name
    retries=3,                          # same params as PythonOperator
    retry_delay=timedelta(minutes=5),
    pool='nordgrid_validation_pool',
    doc_md='Validates meter reading row counts and null percentages.'
)
def validate_readings(file_path: str, min_rows: int = 100_000) -> dict:
    """Check that the readings file meets quality thresholds."""
    row_count = count_rows(file_path)
    null_pct = compute_null_percentage(file_path, 'meter_id')

    return {
        'file_path': file_path,
        'row_count': row_count,
        'null_pct': null_pct,
        'passed': row_count >= min_rows and null_pct < 0.05
    }

The decorator accepts every parameter that PythonOperator accepts: retries, retry_delay, pool, priority_weight, execution_timeout, queue, and more. If you omit task_id, Airflow uses the function name. The function's return value is automatically serialized and stored in XCom.
Parse Time vs Execution Time

This distinction matters because it explains a common source of confusion. At parse time - every 30 seconds when the scheduler reads your DAG file - Airflow sees the @task decorator and creates a task object. The function body does not execute. At execution time - when the scheduler assigns the task to a worker - the function body runs, receives its arguments from upstream XCom, and its return value is stored.

blocks.title: Common mistake
blocks.content: Do not put slow operations (API calls, database queries, heavy imports) outside the @task function body. Code at module level runs every scheduler parse cycle. Only code inside the decorated function runs at execution time.
Return Value Serialization

When your function returns a value, Airflow serializes it to JSON and stores it in the XCom table of the metadata database. This means your return values must be JSON-serializable: strings, numbers, booleans, lists, dicts, and None. You cannot return DataFrames, file handles, database connections, or custom objects unless you implement custom serialization.

blocks.definition: The process by which TaskFlow return values are converted to JSON and stored in the Airflow metadata database. All values passed between tasks go through this serialization layer.
blocks.analogy: Think of XCom as a message board between shift workers at NordGrid's control room. A departing operator writes a note ('file is at s3://path, 1.2M rows, checksummed') on the board. The next operator reads the note. The board only holds text - you cannot pin a physical turbine to it.

blocks.content: Accessing Template Variables

In classic PythonOperator, you access the execution date through context['ds'] or context['data_interval_start']. In TaskFlow, you declare these as keyword arguments with default None. Airflow recognizes them and injects the values at runtime.

items: ds, ds_nodash, ts, ts_nodash - date/time strings for the logical execution date
data_interval_start, data_interval_end - datetime objects for the data interval boundaries
run_id - the unique identifier for this DAG run
dag_run - the full DagRun object (use sparingly - it is not JSON-serializable)

blocks.title: Production note
blocks.content: Always use data_interval_start and data_interval_end for partition-aware processing. Never use datetime.now() - it breaks idempotency because re-runs and backfills will process the wrong date.

The most powerful feature of TaskFlow is not the decorator itself - it is what happens when you pass the return value of one task as an argument to another. Airflow infers the dependency automatically. No >> operator needed.
How Inference Works

When the DAG parser encounters extract_result = extract_meters() followed by validate_meters(extract_result), it does not execute the functions. Instead, extract_meters() returns an XComArg object - a lazy placeholder that represents 'the future return value of the extract_meters task.' When validate_meters receives this placeholder as an argument, Airflow registers the dependency: validate_meters depends on extract_meters.

blocks.title: TaskFlow dependency inference
blocks.content:
extract_meters()  ->  returns XComArg (placeholder)
        |
validate_meters(XComArg)  ->  Airflow registers dependency
        |
load_to_silver(XComArg)   ->  Airflow registers dependency

Result: extract >> validate >> load  (inferred, not declared)
blocks.caption: At parse time, function calls create XComArg objects. Airflow reads these to build the dependency graph without any explicit >> declarations.

blocks.content: When You Still Need >>

Dependency inference only works when data flows between tasks. If two tasks must run in sequence but do not exchange data, Airflow cannot infer the dependency from function calls alone. In that case, you still use the >> operator.

blocks.title: Design principle
blocks.content: If data flows between tasks, let TaskFlow infer the dependency. If only execution order matters (no data exchange), use >>. Mixing both in the same DAG is normal and expected.
Diamond Dependencies

TaskFlow handles diamond patterns naturally. If task C depends on the outputs of both task A and task B, you simply pass both outputs as arguments. Airflow infers that C depends on A and B, and will not run C until both complete.

Real tasks rarely return a single value. NordGrid's extract task needs to communicate a file path, a row count, a checksum, and a timestamp to downstream tasks. TaskFlow provides clean patterns for structured return values and selective downstream access.
Returning Dictionaries
The most common pattern is returning a dictionary. Each key becomes an independently accessible XCom value that downstream tasks can pick from selectively.

blocks.definition: When set on @task, Airflow stores each dictionary key as a separate XCom entry rather than storing the entire dictionary as one value. This allows downstream tasks to depend on individual keys using result['key'] syntax.
blocks.analogy: Instead of writing one long note on the control room board ('file at path X, 1.2M rows, checksum Y, extracted at time Z'), the operator writes four separate sticky notes - one per fact. The next operator can grab just the ones they need.

blocks.content: Without multiple_outputs

If you omit multiple_outputs=True, the entire dictionary is stored as a single XCom value. Downstream tasks receive the full dictionary and must unpack it themselves. This still works, but it means every downstream task depends on the entire return value - you lose the selective access pattern.

blocks.title: When to use each
blocks.content: Use multiple_outputs=True when different downstream tasks need different subsets of the return value. Omit it when the return value is a single logical unit (like a file path string) or when only one downstream task consumes it.
XCom Size Limits

Remember that XCom is stored in the metadata database. The default backend has a practical limit of about 48KB per XCom value (depending on the database). Return references - file paths, table names, S3 URIs - not the data itself. Never return a DataFrame or a large list of records through XCom.

blocks.title: Anti-pattern
blocks.content: Never return actual data rows through XCom. Return a reference to where the data lives. Bad: return df.to_dict(). Good: return 's3://nordgrid-silver/meters/2026-03-15.parquet'. The data lives in storage. XCom carries the address.

TaskFlow does not replace operators - it complements them. Sensors, transfer operators, and custom operators still serve critical roles that @task functions cannot fill. The real power comes from mixing both in the same DAG, choosing whichever is clearest for each step.
When to Use @task vs Operators

rows: Clean, testable, return values flow naturally
Waiting for a file or condition
Handles connections, retries, streaming large files
Running a Bash command or script
Handles API auth, polling, status checking
Business logic specific to your company
Use @task for one-off logic, custom operator for reusable patterns

blocks.content: Wiring TaskFlow to Operators

The key to mixing is understanding that @task functions return XComArg objects, and most operator parameters accept XComArg as input. This means you can pass a TaskFlow return value directly into an operator parameter.

from airflow.decorators import dag, task
from airflow.sensors.filesystem import FileSensor
from airflow.providers.amazon.aws.transfers.local_to_s3 import LocalFilesystemToS3Operator
from datetime import datetime

@dag(schedule='@daily', start_date=datetime(2026, 1, 1), catchup=False)
def nordgrid_meter_pipeline():

    # Sensor: wait for the meter file to land on local disk
    wait_for_file = FileSensor(
        task_id='wait_for_meters',
        filepath='/data/incoming/meters_{{ ds_nodash }}.csv',
        poke_interval=300,
        timeout=7200,
        mode='reschedule'
    )

    # TaskFlow: validate the file
    @task
    def validate_file(ds_nodash=None) -> dict:
        path = f'/data/incoming/meters_{ds_nodash}.csv'
        row_count = count_rows(path)
        if row_count < 100_000:
            raise ValueError(f'Only {row_count} rows in {path}')
        return {'local_path': path, 'row_count': row_count}

    # TaskFlow: generate S3 destination
    @task
    def build_s3_path(ds_nodash=None) -> str:
        return f's3://nordgrid-bronze/meters/{ds_nodash}/readings.csv'

    # Operator: upload to S3
    upload = LocalFilesystemToS3Operator(
        task_id='upload_to_s3',
        filename="{{ ti.xcom_pull(task_ids='validate_file', key='local_path') }}",
        dest_key=build_s3_path(),  # XComArg from TaskFlow!
        aws_conn_id='aws_nordgrid'
    )

    # Wire: sensor >> taskflow >> operator
    metadata = validate_file()
    wait_for_file >> metadata >> upload

nordgrid_meter_pipeline()

blocks.title: Hybrid pattern
blocks.content: The most effective production DAGs mix all three: sensors for waiting, @task for business logic, operators for system integration. Do not force everything into @task just because it looks cleaner. Use the right tool for each step.
XComArg in Operator Parameters

Not every operator parameter accepts XComArg directly. Jinja-templated parameters (those listed in the operator's template_fields) work with the {{ ti.xcom_pull() }} pattern. Non-templated parameters require the XComArg to be passed as a Python object, which only works if the parameter is evaluated at runtime, not parse time.

blocks.title: Common pitfall
blocks.content: If you pass an XComArg to a non-templated operator parameter, the operator receives the XComArg placeholder object - not the actual value. Check the operator's template_fields before wiring. When in doubt, use a Jinja template string with xcom_pull().

As DAGs grow, the graph view becomes cluttered. NordGrid's Silver pipeline has twelve validation tasks, four transformation tasks, and three quality gates. Without visual grouping, the UI is a wall of boxes. Task Groups solve this by collapsing related tasks into named sections.
The @task_group Decorator
Just as @task decorates a function to create a task, @task_group decorates a function to create a collapsible group of tasks. Every @task defined inside the group function belongs to that group.
What the UI Shows

In the Airflow graph view, the quality_gates group appears as a single collapsible box. Click it to expand and see the four tasks inside. This keeps the top-level view clean: extract - quality_gates - transform. The complexity is there when you need it, hidden when you do not.

blocks.title: Task Group UI representation
blocks.content:
Top level (collapsed):
  [extract_bronze_paths] -> [quality_gates] -> [transform_to_silver]

Expanded:
  [extract_bronze_paths] -> quality_gates group:
                              [validate_meters]  -->
                              [validate_scada]   --> [aggregate_results]
                              [validate_weather] -->
                           -> [transform_to_silver]
blocks.caption: Task Groups collapse related tasks into a single visual unit. Click to expand and see the internal structure.

blocks.content: Nesting Groups

Task Groups can be nested. For a complex Silver pipeline, you might have a top-level quality_gates group containing a meters_validation subgroup and a scada_validation subgroup. Keep nesting to two levels maximum - deeper nesting makes the UI confusing rather than helpful.

blocks.title: Naming convention
blocks.content: Use group_id names that describe the phase, not the implementation: 'quality_gates' rather than 'validation_tasks', 'bronze_ingestion' rather than 'sftp_downloads'. The group name appears in the UI and in log paths.

When a TaskFlow function raises an exception, Airflow treats it exactly like a failed PythonOperator task: the task is marked as failed, retries are attempted according to the retry policy, and failure callbacks fire. But there are subtleties in how you should handle errors inside @task functions versus relying on Airflow's retry machinery.
Let Airflow Retry vs Handle Yourself

The decision is simple: if the error is transient (network timeout, API rate limit, temporary file lock), let Airflow retry. If the error is a data quality issue that retrying will not fix (null percentage too high, row count too low, schema mismatch), raise an exception immediately with a clear message.

rows: SFTP connection refused - retry in 5 minutes
Handle in code, return partial result
Region EAST-3 missing but 11 others succeeded
Raise immediately - retrying will not help

blocks.content: The on_failure_callback Pattern

def notify_on_failure(context):
    """Send Slack alert with failure details."""
    task_instance = context['task_instance']
    dag_id = context['dag'].dag_id
    task_id = task_instance.task_id
    exception = context.get('exception', 'Unknown')
    log_url = task_instance.log_url

    message = (
        f":red_circle: *Task Failed*\n"
        f"DAG: {dag_id}\n"
        f"Task: {task_id}\n"
        f"Error: {exception}\n"
        f"Log: {log_url}"
    )
    post_to_slack(channel='#nordgrid-alerts', text=message)

@task(on_failure_callback=notify_on_failure, retries=3)
def validate_meters(file_path: str) -> dict:
    row_count = count_rows(file_path)
    if row_count < 100_000:
        raise ValueError(
            f"Row count {row_count} below 100K threshold in {file_path}. "
            f"Check source system for data delivery issues."
        )
    return {'file_path': file_path, 'row_count': row_count, 'passed': True}

blocks.title: Common mistake
blocks.content: Do not wrap your entire function body in try/except and silently return None on error. This makes the task 'succeed' when it actually failed. Airflow cannot retry what it thinks succeeded. Let real failures propagate as exceptions.
AirflowSkipException

Sometimes you want a task to be skipped rather than failed - for example, when a region has no data for a given day and that is expected behavior, not an error. Raising AirflowSkipException marks the task as 'skipped' in the UI (yellow, not red) and downstream tasks follow the trigger rule logic.

Python type hints do not change TaskFlow's behavior at runtime - Airflow does not enforce types. But they dramatically improve code readability, IDE autocomplete, and team communication. In a production codebase with 60 DAGs and 15 engineers, type hints are not optional.
Basic Type Annotations
Why TypedDict

TypedDict gives you a structured definition of what a task returns. IDE tools like mypy and PyCharm can now flag errors: if you typo result['flie_path'] instead of result['file_path'], the type checker catches it before runtime. This is especially valuable in multi-team environments where one team produces data and another consumes it.

blocks.title: Team standard
blocks.content: For NordGrid's codebase: every @task with multiple_outputs=True must define a TypedDict for its return type. This is enforced by the linting rules in CI. The TypedDict serves as the contract between producer and consumer tasks.
Limitations

items: Airflow does not validate types at runtime. If you annotate a return as int but return a string, Airflow will not raise an error - the downstream task receives a string.
Type hints on template variables (ds_nodash, data_interval_start) are informational only. Airflow injects the correct type regardless of your annotation.
Complex types (Optional, Union, List[Dict]) work with type checkers but have no effect on Airflow's XCom serialization.
TypedDict requires Python 3.8+. All modern Airflow versions support this.

blocks.content: NordGrid has 40 DAGs built with the classic PythonOperator pattern during the beginner track. Converting them all to TaskFlow at once is impractical. This lesson gives you a systematic conversion procedure that you can apply one DAG at a time without breaking production.
The Conversion Procedure

items: Identify the PythonOperator tasks in the DAG. List which ones push/pull XCom and which do not exchange data.
Convert each PythonOperator function to a @task function. Replace xcom_push with return values. Replace xcom_pull with function parameters.
Replace PythonOperator instantiations with direct function calls inside the @dag function.
Remove >> declarations where data flow is now implicit. Keep >> for execution-order-only dependencies.
Convert non-Python operators (sensors, transfers) to remain as operators. Wire them to TaskFlow using >> or XComArg.
Replace the DAG context manager with @dag decorator.
Run the existing DAG integrity tests to verify the conversion is structurally correct.
Run the existing unit tests against the new @task functions (they should pass without modification if the logic is unchanged).
Deploy to staging and trigger a test run. Compare task states and XCom values to the classic version.

blocks.content: Before and After: Meter Pipeline

Here is the NordGrid beginner-track meter pipeline converted step by step. The classic version has three PythonOperators with manual XCom. The TaskFlow version has three @task functions with automatic data flow.
Common Pitfalls During Conversion

items: Forgetting to call the @dag function at module level. Without the final nordgrid_meter_daily() call, Airflow never registers the DAG.
Changing task_id names during conversion. If a task was called 'extract_meters' in the classic version, keep the same name in TaskFlow to preserve run history and XCom references.
Converting sensors to @task. Sensors should stay as operators - they have built-in poke/reschedule logic that @task does not replicate.
Breaking existing downstream DAGs. If another DAG uses ExternalTaskSensor to wait for a specific task_id in this DAG, changing the task_id will break the sensor.

blocks.title: Migration strategy
blocks.content: Convert one DAG at a time, starting with the simplest. Deploy to staging, run a full comparison test, then promote to production. Do not attempt a batch conversion of all 40 DAGs - the risk of cascading breakage is too high.

You now understand the TaskFlow API - the modern interface for writing Airflow DAGs. This lesson reviews the key decisions and gives you a framework for choosing between TaskFlow and classic operators in production.
The Decision Framework

rows: Does it exchange data with other tasks?
Is it waiting for a condition?
Is it a reusable pattern across DAGs?
@task for simple, custom operator for complex
Is it triggering an external system?

blocks.content: What You Now Know

items: The @task decorator transforms Python functions into Airflow tasks with automatic XCom handling
Dependencies are inferred from function call chains - no >> needed when data flows between tasks
multiple_outputs=True splits dictionary returns into separate XCom entries for selective downstream access
TaskFlow and classic operators coexist naturally - sensors wait, @task validates, operators transfer
@task_group collapses related tasks into visual groups for cleaner graph views
Error handling follows the same Airflow retry machinery - let transient errors retry, raise immediately on data quality failures
Type hints with TypedDict create contracts between producer and consumer tasks
Converting classic DAGs follows a systematic 9-step procedure - one DAG at a time, preserving task IDs

blocks.content: Hands-On Exercise

Convert the weather forecast DAG from the beginner capstone to TaskFlow. The original DAG uses PythonOperator for API extraction, validation, and loading, with an HttpSensor waiting for the weather API to be available. Your conversion should:

items: Replace the three PythonOperator tasks with @task functions
Keep the HttpSensor as-is (sensors stay as operators)
Use multiple_outputs=True on the extraction task to return both the forecast data path and the station count
Add a TypedDict for the extraction return type
Group the validation tasks into a @task_group called 'quality_checks'
Add an on_failure_callback that formats a Slack notification with the DAG name, task name, and error message
Run the existing DAG integrity test to verify the conversion

blocks.title: Looking ahead
blocks.content: In Module 2, you will build custom operators and hooks that encapsulate NordGrid's reusable patterns - the SFTP download, the API pull, the quality gate. TaskFlow handles the glue logic between these operators. Together, they form the foundation of a production DAG library.

chapters.title: Custom Operators and Hooks – Building the Toolkit
chapters.description: Design, build, test, and package custom operators and hooks that encapsulate reusable business logic for production DAG libraries.

blocks.content: NordGrid's codebase has a problem. Eight DAGs download files from the company's SFTP server. Each one reimplements the same logic: connect to SFTP, list files matching a date pattern, download the matching file, validate the file size, log metadata, and return the local path. The implementations are almost identical – but not quite. Small variations have crept in over time: one DAG validates size in bytes, another in rows. One retries three times, another retries once. One logs to a structured format, another logs raw strings.
The Copy-Paste Tax

Every copy-pasted implementation creates a maintenance liability. When the SFTP server migrates to a new host, you must update eight DAGs. When the team decides to standardize logging, you must find and fix eight slightly different log statements. When a new engineer joins, they copy the nearest example – including whatever bugs it carries.

Custom operators solve this by encapsulating the pattern once. Instead of eight implementations of 'download from SFTP and validate,' you build NordGridSFTPOperator – a single class that handles connection management, date pattern matching, file validation, structured logging, and error handling. Every DAG instantiates the operator with its specific parameters.

blocks.title: Before and after custom operators
blocks.content:
BEFORE: Copy-paste pattern
  dag_bronze_meters  → [SFTP logic v1, 40 lines]
  dag_bronze_scada   → [SFTP logic v2, 35 lines]
  dag_bronze_weather → [SFTP logic v3, 42 lines]
  dag_bronze_pricing → [SFTP logic v1 (copied), 40 lines]
  ... 4 more DAGs with slight variations

AFTER: Custom operator
  dag_bronze_meters  → NordGridSFTPOperator(file_pattern='meters_{{ ds_nodash }}.csv')
  dag_bronze_scada   → NordGridSFTPOperator(file_pattern='scada_{{ ds_nodash }}.parquet')
  dag_bronze_weather → NordGridSFTPOperator(file_pattern='weather_{{ ds_nodash }}.json')
  dag_bronze_pricing → NordGridSFTPOperator(file_pattern='pricing_{{ ds_nodash }}.csv')
  ... same operator, different parameters
blocks.caption: Custom operators replace copy-pasted logic with a single parameterized class. Bug fixes and improvements propagate to all DAGs automatically.

blocks.content: When to Build a Custom Operator

rows: Weather API with OAuth, rate limiting, pagination
One-off logic used in one DAG
Ad-hoc data cleanup for a specific migration
Built-in operator does 90% of what you need

blocks.title: The 3-DAG rule
blocks.content: If you write the same pattern in three or more DAGs, extract it into a custom operator. Two is a coincidence. Three is a pattern. This rule keeps you from over-engineering while ensuring real patterns get proper abstraction.

Every operator in Airflow – from PythonOperator to SparkSubmitOperator – inherits from BaseOperator. Understanding what BaseOperator provides and what your custom class must implement is the foundation for building reliable operators.
The BaseOperator Contract

BaseOperator gives you task lifecycle management (retries, timeouts, callbacks), UI integration (color, icon, documentation), template rendering (Jinja substitution before execution), and XCom (return values stored automatically). In exchange, your class must implement one method: execute(self, context).

from airflow.models import BaseOperator
from airflow.utils.context import Context

class NordGridSFTPOperator(BaseOperator):
    """
    Downloads a file from NordGrid's SFTP server,
    validates file size, and returns the local path.
    """

    # UI customization
    ui_color = '#1B3A4B'
    ui_fgcolor = '#FFFFFF'

    # Fields that support Jinja templating
    template_fields = ('file_pattern', 'remote_dir', 'local_dir')

    def __init__(
        self,
        *,
        sftp_conn_id: str = 'sftp_nordgrid',
        file_pattern: str,
        remote_dir: str = '/data/outgoing/',
        local_dir: str = '/tmp/airflow/',
        min_file_bytes: int = 1024,
        **kwargs
    ):
        super().__init__(**kwargs)
        self.sftp_conn_id = sftp_conn_id
        self.file_pattern = file_pattern
        self.remote_dir = remote_dir
        self.local_dir = local_dir
        self.min_file_bytes = min_file_bytes

    def execute(self, context: Context) -> str:
        """Download file from SFTP, validate, return local path."""
        from airflow.providers.sftp.hooks.sftp import SFTPHook

        hook = SFTPHook(ssh_conn_id=self.sftp_conn_id)
        remote_path = f"{self.remote_dir}{self.file_pattern}"
        local_path = f"{self.local_dir}{self.file_pattern}"

        self.log.info(f"Downloading {remote_path} to {local_path}")
        hook.retrieve_file(remote_path, local_path)

        # Validate
        import os
        file_size = os.path.getsize(local_path)
        if file_size < self.min_file_bytes:
            raise ValueError(
                f"File {local_path} is {file_size} bytes, "
                f"below minimum {self.min_file_bytes}"
            )

        self.log.info(f"Downloaded {file_size:,} bytes to {local_path}")
        return local_path  # automatically pushed to XCom
Key Design Decisions

items: __init__ stores configuration. It runs at DAG parse time – every 30 seconds. Never put slow operations (API calls, file I/O, imports) in __init__. Store parameters only.

execute does the work. It runs on a worker at execution time – once per task instance. All slow operations belong here. The context dictionary gives you access to the DAG run, task instance, template variables, and more.

Imports inside execute. Notice that SFTPHook and os are imported inside execute(), not at module level. This prevents heavy imports from slowing the scheduler parse cycle. This is a production best practice for all custom operators.
Return value becomes XCom. Whatever execute() returns is automatically stored as the task's XCom return_value. Downstream tasks can access it with xcom_pull() or TaskFlow argument passing.
self.log is provided by BaseOperator. Use it instead of print(). It integrates with Airflow's log system, appears in the task log UI, and supports structured logging.

blocks.content: Parse Time vs Execution Time

left.label: Parse Time (__init__)
points: No API calls, no file I/O

right.label: Execution Time (execute)
points: Runs once when task is scheduled
API calls, file I/O, heavy imports

blocks.title: Anti-pattern
blocks.content: Never call get_connection(), read files, or make API calls in __init__. If your operator takes 2 seconds to construct, and you have 60 DAGs each with 10 operators, the scheduler spends 20 minutes per parse cycle just constructing operators. This cripples the entire Airflow instance.

Let us build the full NordGridSFTPOperator step by step – not just the download logic, but the production features that make it reliable: connection management via hooks, file pattern matching with glob support, size and age validation, structured metadata logging, and graceful handling of missing files.
The Full Implementation

from airflow.models import BaseOperator
from airflow.utils.context import Context
from airflow.exceptions import AirflowSkipException
from typing import Optional, Sequence
import os


class NordGridSFTPOperator(BaseOperator):
    """
    Download a file from NordGrid's SFTP server with validation.
    """

    ui_color = '#1B3A4B'
    ui_fgcolor = '#FFFFFF'
    template_fields: Sequence[str] = (
        'file_pattern', 'remote_dir', 'local_dir'
    )

    def __init__(
        self,
        *,
        sftp_conn_id: str = 'sftp_nordgrid',
        file_pattern: str,
        remote_dir: str = '/data/outgoing/',
        local_dir: str = '/tmp/airflow/downloads/',
        min_file_bytes: int = 1024,
        skip_if_missing: bool = False,
        **kwargs
    ):
        super().__init__(**kwargs)
        self.sftp_conn_id = sftp_conn_id
        self.file_pattern = file_pattern
        self.remote_dir = remote_dir
        self.local_dir = local_dir
        self.min_file_bytes = min_file_bytes
        self.skip_if_missing = skip_if_missing

    def execute(self, context: Context) -> str:
        from airflow.providers.sftp.hooks.sftp import SFTPHook
        import time

        hook = SFTPHook(ssh_conn_id=self.sftp_conn_id)
        remote_path = os.path.join(self.remote_dir, self.file_pattern)

        try:
            file_info = hook.get_mod_time(remote_path)
        except FileNotFoundError:
            if self.skip_if_missing:
                raise AirflowSkipException(
                    f"File {remote_path} not found – skipping"
                )
            raise FileNotFoundError(
                f"Expected file {remote_path} not found on SFTP."
            )

        os.makedirs(self.local_dir, exist_ok=True)
        local_path = os.path.join(self.local_dir, self.file_pattern)
        start = time.time()
        hook.retrieve_file(remote_path, local_path)
        duration = time.time() - start

        file_size = os.path.getsize(local_path)
        if file_size < self.min_file_bytes:
            os.remove(local_path)
            raise ValueError(
                f"File {self.file_pattern} is {file_size:,} bytes, "
                f"below minimum {self.min_file_bytes:,}."
            )

        self.log.info(
            "SFTP download complete",
            extra={'file': self.file_pattern, 'bytes': file_size, 'seconds': round(duration, 2)}
        )
        return local_path
Usage in a DAG

from nordgrid_plugins.operators.sftp_operator import NordGridSFTPOperator

download_meters = NordGridSFTPOperator(
    task_id='download_meters',
    file_pattern='meters_{{ ds_nodash }}.csv',
    remote_dir='/data/outgoing/meters/',
    min_file_bytes=50_000_000,
    skip_if_missing=False,
    retries=3,
    retry_delay=timedelta(minutes=5)
)

blocks.title: Key design choices
blocks.content: The skip_if_missing parameter lets the same operator serve both mandatory sources (meters – must exist or fail) and optional sources (substations – skip gracefully if not updated today). This eliminates two separate operator classes.
Detailed Error Messages

Notice that every error message includes actionable context: which file, what threshold, and what likely went wrong. At 3 AM when an on-call engineer gets paged, the error message is far more useful than a generic 'ValueError: file too small.'

blocks.title: Production note
blocks.content: Write error messages for the person who will read them at 3 AM. Include the file name, the actual value, the expected threshold, and a suggested cause.

Operators do work. Hooks manage connections. This separation is fundamental to Airflow's architecture. A hook encapsulates how to connect to an external system – authentication, connection pooling, retries, and session management. An operator uses a hook to interact with that system without knowing the connection details.
Hooks vs Operators

left.label: Hook
points: Knows how to authenticate and connect
Not a task – invisible in the DAG

right.label: Operator
points: Knows what to do once connected
Uses one or more hooks internally
A task – visible in the DAG graph

blocks.content: The NordGridSFTPOperator from Lesson 3 already uses this pattern: it calls SFTPHook to manage the SSH/SFTP connection.
Building NordGridAPIHook
NordGrid's weather API requires OAuth2 authentication, respects rate limits (60 requests per minute), and returns paginated JSON responses.

from airflow.hooks.base import BaseHook
import requests
import time
from typing import Any, Optional


class NordGridAPIHook(BaseHook):
    conn_name_attr = 'api_conn_id'
    default_conn_name = 'nordgrid_weather_api'
    conn_type = 'http'
    hook_name = 'NordGrid Weather API'

    def __init__(self, api_conn_id='nordgrid_weather_api', max_requests_per_minute=60):
        super().__init__()
        self.api_conn_id = api_conn_id
        self.max_rpm = max_requests_per_minute
        self._token = None
        self._token_expires = 0
        self._request_times = []

    def get_conn(self):
        conn = self.get_connection(self.api_conn_id)
        session = requests.Session()
        session.headers['Authorization'] = f'Bearer {self._get_token(conn)}'
        return session

    def _get_token(self, conn):
        if self._token and time.time() < self._token_expires:
            return self._token
        resp = requests.post(f'https://{conn.host}/oauth/token', data={
            'grant_type': 'client_credentials',
            'client_id': conn.login,
            'client_secret': conn.password
        }, timeout=30)
        resp.raise_for_status()
        data = resp.json()
        self._token = data['access_token']
        self._token_expires = time.time() + data.get('expires_in', 3600) - 60
        return self._token

    def _rate_limit(self):
        now = time.time()
        self._request_times = [t for t in self._request_times if now - t < 60]
        if len(self._request_times) >= self.max_rpm:
            sleep_time = 60 - (now - self._request_times[0])
            self.log.info(f'Rate limit: sleeping {sleep_time:.1f}s')
            time.sleep(sleep_time)
        self._request_times.append(time.time())
Connection Resolution
The hook calls self.get_connection(self.api_conn_id) to retrieve the Connection object from Airflow's metadata database. No credentials appear in the DAG code or the hook code.

blocks.definition: An Airflow object that stores the credentials and endpoint information for an external system. Connections are managed through the UI, CLI, environment variables, or a Secrets Backend.
blocks.analogy: A Connection is like a keycard at NordGrid's facility. The keycard grants access to a specific room. The worker uses the keycard without knowing the lock's mechanism.

blocks.title: Common mistake
blocks.content: Do not hardcode credentials in hooks. Always use self.get_connection().

Template fields are what make operators dynamic. When you write file_pattern='meters_{{ ds_nodash }}.csv', the {{ ds_nodash }} is a Jinja2 template that Airflow renders with the execution context before calling execute().
How Template Rendering Works

items: At parse time, __init__ stores the raw string 'meters_{{ ds_nodash }}.csv'.
Before execute() runs, Airflow checks template_fields.
For each listed field, Airflow renders the Jinja2 template.
The rendered value replaces the raw string.
execute() runs with all template fields already resolved.

blocks.content: Template Variables Available

blocks.title: Power pattern
blocks.content: Template fields are not limited to dates. You can template S3 bucket names, connection hosts, or conditional paths from params. This makes operators environment-agnostic.

blocks.title: Inheritance tip
blocks.content: When extending an operator, concatenate the parent's template_fields: template_fields = ParentOperator.template_fields + ('new_field',).
Every Silver-layer DAG at NordGrid needs a quality gate: check the row count, null percentage, freshness, and schema before promoting data.
The Quality Gate Contract
The operator accepts a data source reference, thresholds, and a mode (fail or warn). It produces a structured report stored in XCom.

from airflow.models import BaseOperator
from airflow.utils.context import Context
from typing import Sequence
from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict

@dataclass
class QualityReport:
    source: str
    row_count: int
    null_pct: float
    freshness_hours: float
    checks_passed: int
    checks_failed: int
    passed: bool
    details: list

class NordGridQualityGateOperator(BaseOperator):
    ui_color = '#E8A838'
    template_fields: Sequence[str] = ('check_query', 'source_name')

    def __init__(self, *, source_name, check_query, db_conn_id='postgres_nordgrid',
                 min_rows=1000, max_null_pct=0.05, max_freshness_hours=24.0,
                 fail_on_violation=True, **kwargs):
        super().__init__(**kwargs)
        self.source_name = source_name
        self.check_query = check_query
        self.db_conn_id = db_conn_id
        self.min_rows = min_rows
        self.max_null_pct = max_null_pct
        self.max_freshness_hours = max_freshness_hours
        self.fail_on_violation = fail_on_violation

    def execute(self, context: Context) -> dict:
        from airflow.providers.common.sql.hooks.sql import DbApiHook
        from datetime import datetime, timezone
        hook = DbApiHook.get_hook(self.db_conn_id)
        records = hook.get_records(self.check_query)
        row = records[0]
        row_count, null_count, total_count, max_ts = row
        null_pct = null_count / total_count if total_count > 0 else 1.0
        freshness = ((datetime.now(timezone.utc) - max_ts).total_seconds() / 3600 if max_ts else float('inf'))
        details, failures = [], 0
        if row_count < self.min_rows: details.append(f'Row count {row_count:,} < {self.min_rows:,}'); failures += 1
        if null_pct > self.max_null_pct: details.append(f'Null pct {null_pct:.2%} > {self.max_null_pct:.2%}'); failures += 1
        if freshness > self.max_freshness_hours: details.append(f'Freshness {freshness:.1f}h > {self.max_freshness_hours}h'); failures += 1
        report = QualityReport(source=self.source_name, row_count=row_count, null_pct=round(null_pct,4),
                               freshness_hours=round(freshness,2), checks_passed=3-failures,
                               checks_failed=failures, passed=failures==0, details=details)
        if not report.passed and self.fail_on_violation:
            raise ValueError(f'Quality gate failed for {self.source_name}: ' + '; '.join(details))
        return asdict(report)

blocks.title: Tightening standards over time
blocks.content: Start with loose thresholds and tighten them as you understand your data. Because thresholds are operator parameters, tightening standards is a one-line change per DAG.
Not every custom operator needs to be built from BaseOperator. If an existing operator does 80% of what you need, extend it.
Extending S3KeySensor

from airflow.providers.amazon.aws.sensors.s3 import S3KeySensor
from airflow.utils.context import Context
from datetime import datetime, timezone

class NordGridS3Sensor(S3KeySensor):
    template_fields = S3KeySensor.template_fields + ('expected_by',)

    def __init__(self, *, expected_by=None, **kwargs):
        super().__init__(**kwargs)
        self.expected_by = expected_by
        self._first_poke_time = None

    def poke(self, context: Context) -> bool:
        if self._first_poke_time is None:
            self._first_poke_time = datetime.now(timezone.utc)
        result = super().poke(context)
        if result:
            wait_seconds = (datetime.now(timezone.utc) - self._first_poke_time).total_seconds()
            self.log.info("File arrived", extra={'wait_seconds': round(wait_seconds, 1)})
        return result
When to Inherit vs Build Fresh

rows: Inheritance would fight the parent's design

blocks.title: Inheritance pitfall
blocks.content: When the parent operator's version changes (Airflow provider upgrade), your overridden methods may break. Pin provider versions.
The Mixin Pattern
A custom operator packaged as an installable Python module is useful for the entire team.
Package Structure

blocks.title: nordgrid-airflow-plugins package layout
blocks.content:
nordgrid-airflow-plugins/
├── nordgrid_plugins/
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── operators/
│   │   ├── __init__.py
│   │   ├── sftp_operator.py
│   │   ├── quality_gate_operator.py
│   ├── hooks/
│       ├── __init__.py
│       ├── api_hook.py
├── tests/
│   ├── test_sftp_operator.py
│   ├── test_quality_gate.py
├── setup.py
├── requirements.txt
blocks.caption: A standard Python package with operators, hooks, and tests.

blocks.content: The setup.py

from setuptools import setup, find_packages

setup(
    name='nordgrid-airflow-plugins',
    version='2.1.0',
    packages=find_packages(exclude=['tests']),
    python_requires='>=3.9',
    install_requires=[
        'apache-airflow>=2.8.0',
        'apache-airflow-providers-sftp>=4.0.0',
    ],
)
Why Not the Plugins Folder?
Airflow has a legacy plugins/ folder. Avoid it for custom operators. A proper Python package with setup.py provides versioning, dependency management, and test infrastructure.

blocks.title: Versioning strategy
blocks.content: Use semantic versioning: MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH. Pin the version in your DAG repository's requirements.txt.
Custom operators are only valuable if they are testable. Because operators are plain Python classes, you can test them with standard pytest.
The Testing Strategy
Test the operator's logic, not Airflow's machinery. Mock the context object and hooks.

blocks.title: Test coverage targets
blocks.content: Every custom operator should have: one test for the happy path, one test per error condition, one test per configuration flag.
You now understand how to build, package, and test custom operators and hooks.
The Operator/Hook Separation

items: Hooks manage connections – authentication, session lifecycle, and credential resolution
Operators execute business logic – they use hooks to interact with external systems
template_fields make operators dynamic
Custom operators encapsulate reusable patterns – the 3-DAG rule
Inheritance extends existing operators
Python packages make operators shareable
Unit tests mock hooks and context

blocks.content: What You Built

rows: Download and validate files from SFTP

blocks.content: Hands-On Exercise
Build NordGridNotificationOperator – a reusable operator that sends structured Slack notifications.

items: Accept parameters: channel, severity, message_template (Jinja-enabled), include_log_link.
Use Airflow's SlackHook to send the message.
Format with DAG name, task name, execution date, duration, and log link.
Support severity-based emoji prefixes.
List message_template in template_fields.
Write three unit tests.
Add to the nordgrid-airflow-plugins package.

blocks.title: Looking ahead
blocks.content: In Module 3, you will use these custom operators inside dynamically mapped tasks.

chapters.title: Advanced Dynamic Tasks – Pipelines That Scale Themselves
chapters.description: Build pipelines that automatically adapt to variable inputs using dynamic task mapping, mapped task groups, and fan-out/fan-in patterns.

blocks.content: In the beginner track, NordGrid had four regions: NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, WEST. You built four tasks – one per region – and wired them into a diamond pattern. It worked. Then NordGrid expanded to twelve regions. Now you need to add eight more tasks, copy-paste the logic, update the dependency chain, and redeploy. Next quarter they add three more regions. The quarter after that, they merge two regions into one.

Every time the region list changes, someone edits DAG code. Every edit risks introducing a bug. Every redeployment risks breaking a running pipeline. This is the hardcoding problem – and dynamic tasks are the solution.
What Hardcoding Looks Like
What Dynamic Looks Like

blocks.title: The core idea
blocks.content: Write the logic once. Let the data decide how many times it runs. Adding a region means adding a row to a database table – not editing Python code, not redeploying a DAG.
The NordGrid Growth Story

The .expand() method is the engine of dynamic task mapping. It takes one idea – 'run this task once for each item in a list' – and handles all the complexity: creating the right number of task instances, passing each item to its instance, tracking success and failure independently, and storing results in XCom.
The Simplest Example
What Happens Step by Step

items: Parse time (every 30 seconds): Airflow reads your DAG file and sees .expand(). It creates a single 'mapped task' placeholder. It does NOT know how many instances there will be yet.
Scheduling time (when the DAG run starts): The upstream task runs and returns the list. Airflow now knows the list length. It creates N task instances.
Execution time (when each instance runs): Each task instance runs independently on a worker. Each has its own logs, its own XCom, and its own success/failure status.

blocks.title: The key mental model
blocks.content: Think of .expand() like a copy machine. You write one original document (the task function). At runtime, the copy machine makes N copies – one per item in the list. Each copy gets stamped with its own item and works independently.
NordGrid: Expanding Over Regions
What the UI Shows

In the Airflow graph view, you see one box labeled 'download_region' with a small badge showing the mapped count (e.g., '12'). Click it to see all 12 instances. Each instance has its own status, logs, and XCom values. You can retry a single failed instance without re-running the others.

blocks.title: Mapped task in the Airflow UI
blocks.content:
Graph View:
  [get_active_regions] → [download_region ×12]

Expanded (click the ×12 badge):
  [download_region [0]: NORTH-1]  ✓
  [download_region [1]: NORTH-2]  ✓
  ...
  [download_region [8]: EAST-3]   ✗ ← retry just this one
  ...
  [download_region [11]: WEST-3]  ✓
blocks.caption: Each mapped instance is independently retriable. One failure does not block the other eleven.

blocks.content: Most real tasks need more than one argument. The .partial() method sets the arguments that stay the same. The .expand() method sets the argument that changes. Together, they create fully configured task instances.
The Concept

blocks.title: Common mistake
blocks.content: You cannot .expand() multiple arguments simultaneously with separate lists. For cartesian products, use expand_kwargs with a pre-built list of all combinations.
expand_kwargs for Complex Inputs

blocks.title: When to use which
blocks.content: One varying argument – use .expand(). One varying + some fixed – use .partial(...).expand(). Multiple varying arguments per instance – use .expand_kwargs(list_of_dicts).

So far, .expand() maps a single task over a list. But what if each region needs three steps – download, validate, transform – and all three should repeat per region? Mapped task groups solve this: they expand an entire group of tasks together.
The Solution: Map the Whole Group

blocks.title: The mental model
blocks.content: A mapped task group is like a franchise. You design one restaurant (the group template). Then you open 12 locations (one per region). Each location operates independently.

blocks.title: Common pitfall
blocks.content: Every task inside the group must receive its inputs from within the group. You cannot have a task inside the group directly reference a task outside the group.
Expanding tasks is the fan-out: one task becomes twelve. But what happens after all twelve finish? You usually need to collect the results. This is the fan-in.
The Basic Pattern

blocks.title: Automatic collection
blocks.content: You do not need any special syntax to collect mapped results. Just pass the mapped output to a regular task. Airflow automatically gathers all instance results into a list.
Handling Partial Failures

rows: Wait for all – fail if any failed

blocks.content: When one of twelve mapped instances fails, what happens to the other eleven? By default, mapped instances are independent. If process_region[8] fails, the other eleven instances continue running.

blocks.definition: Each mapped task instance runs on its own worker, has its own retry counter, and succeeds or fails independently.
blocks.analogy: NordGrid's twelve regional substations operate independently. If EAST-3 trips a breaker, the other eleven keep delivering power.

blocks.content: Retry Per Instance
Controlling Downstream Behavior

rows: Alert task that always sends status

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Use a two-track approach: 'continue with partial results' for daily reports and 'all or nothing' for billing-critical Gold tables.

In the examples so far, get_active_regions() returned a hardcoded list. In production, that list comes from somewhere dynamic: a database query, an API call, a file listing. The expand list is not known until the upstream task actually runs.
How Runtime Lists Work

When you write process_region.expand(region=get_active_regions()), the get_active_regions() call does not execute immediately. At parse time, it returns an XComArg – a placeholder. At execution time, the upstream task runs, returns a list, and Airflow uses that list to determine how many instances to create.

blocks.title: Empty list gotcha
blocks.content: If the upstream task returns an empty list, Airflow creates zero mapped instances. The mapped task is marked as 'skipped'. Use trigger_rule='none_failed_min_one_success' on downstream tasks.

items: List of strings: ['NORTH-1', 'NORTH-2', ...] – simplest and most common
List of integers: [101, 102, 103, ...] – useful for station IDs
List of dicts: [{'region': 'NORTH-1', 'format': 'csv'}, ...] – for expand_kwargs
NOT a set, tuple, or generator – Airflow expects a JSON-serializable list

blocks.content: Real pipelines chain multiple mapped tasks together. When you pass a mapped task's output to another .expand() call, Airflow maintains the one-to-one correspondence.
Mapped Output to Mapped Input

blocks.title: Automatic pairing
blocks.content: When you expand over the output of another mapped task, Airflow pairs them by index. download[0] produces result[0], which feeds into validate[0].

blocks.title: Same length required
blocks.content: When expanding over multiple mapped outputs, all lists must have the same length.
Dynamic tasks are powerful, but they have guardrails and edge cases.
Maximum Mapped Instances
Airflow limits the maximum number of mapped instances per task. The default is 1,024. You can increase this limit in configuration.

blocks.title: Chunking pattern
blocks.content: If you have 10,000 items, do not create 10,000 mapped instances. Chunk into groups of 100 and expand over 100 chunks.
When NOT to Use Dynamic Tasks

rows: You need named tasks in UI
Named tasks if list is small

blocks.title: The sweet spot
blocks.content: Dynamic tasks shine when the list has 5-500 items, changes between runs, and each item needs the same processing.
You now understand how to build pipelines that scale themselves.
The Expand Decision Tree

items: Does the list change between runs? If no, consider regular tasks.
One varying argument or multiple? One – .expand(). Multiple – .expand_kwargs().
Are there fixed arguments too? Yes – .partial(...).expand().
Does each item need multiple steps? Yes – mapped @task_group.
Do you need to combine results? Yes – add a regular task downstream.
Can a partial failure continue? Yes – use trigger_rule='none_failed_min_one_success'.
Is the list very large (500+)? Yes – chunk the items into batches.

blocks.content: What You Now Know

items: .expand() creates one task instance per item in a list
.partial() locks in shared arguments while .expand() varies the dynamic argument
.expand_kwargs() handles cases where each instance needs multiple different arguments
Mapped task groups expand an entire mini-pipeline per item
Fan-in is automatic: pass mapped output to a regular task
Each mapped instance is independent – retries, failures, and XCom are tracked separately
trigger_rule controls whether downstream tasks wait for all instances or proceed with partial results
The sweet spot is 5-500 items; above that, use the chunking pattern

blocks.title: Looking ahead
blocks.content: In Module 4, you will replace the daily schedule trigger with Datasets – event-driven scheduling where the Silver layer automatically starts when all Bronze data is ready.

chapters.title: Advanced Scheduling and Datasets – Event-Driven Orchestration
chapters.description: Replace clock-based scheduling with event-driven orchestration using Datasets and custom timetables.

blocks.content: NordGrid’s meter pipeline runs at 6:00 AM every day because that is when files usually arrive. But 'usually' is not 'always.' Last Monday the files arrived at 5:30 AM – the pipeline waited 30 minutes for no reason. Last Wednesday the files arrived at 7:15 AM – the pipeline started at 6:00, found nothing, and failed.
The root cause is the same in every case: the pipeline is scheduled by the clock, not by the data.
Three Scheduling Problems

rows: Pipeline starts before data is ready, fails
Silver starts on clock, not when ALL sources are fresh

blocks.content: The Solution: Run When Ready
Event-driven scheduling flips the model. Instead of 'run at 6 AM and hope data is there,' you say 'run when data arrives.'

blocks.title: The shift in thinking
blocks.content: Cron answers: 'What time should this run?' Datasets answer: 'What event should trigger this?'
A Dataset in Airflow is a named piece of data that one DAG produces and another DAG consumes. It is not the data itself – it is a label that says 'this data exists and was just updated.'
Creating a Dataset

from airflow.datasets import Dataset

BRONZE_METERS  = Dataset('s3://nordgrid-bronze/meters')
BRONZE_SCADA   = Dataset('s3://nordgrid-bronze/scada')
BRONZE_WEATHER = Dataset('s3://nordgrid-bronze/weather')
BRONZE_PRICING = Dataset('s3://nordgrid-bronze/pricing')
SILVER_CLEAN   = Dataset('s3://nordgrid-silver/clean')

blocks.definition: A named label representing a piece of data in your platform. Producers declare Datasets as 'outlets' on their tasks. Consumers declare Datasets in their 'schedule' parameter.
blocks.analogy: A Dataset is like a mailbox flag. When the meter team finishes processing, they raise the flag. The Silver team watches that flag.

blocks.content: The Producer Side
The Consumer Side

blocks.title: The URI convention
blocks.content: Use URIs that match your actual storage paths. Airflow does not access the URI, but using real paths makes the lineage graph self-documenting.
NordGrid’s Silver layer needs all four Bronze sources before it can run. Multi-Dataset scheduling solves this by requiring ALL specified Datasets to be fresh before triggering.
AND Logic: Wait for All
OR Logic: Trigger on Any

blocks.title: Choosing AND vs OR
blocks.content: Use AND (list) when the downstream needs ALL inputs. Use OR (| operator) when the downstream should react to EACH input independently.
Airflow’s web UI includes a Datasets view that shows the lineage graph: which DAGs produce which Datasets, and which DAGs consume them.
Debugging: Why Did My DAG Not Trigger?

items: Open the Datasets view and find the consumer DAG.
Check which Datasets it requires.
For each Dataset, check the 'Last Updated' timestamp.
If one Dataset has not updated, find the producer DAG and check its status.
Common cause: the producer DAG succeeded but the outlet task failed or was skipped.

blocks.title: Common gotcha
blocks.content: The Dataset event fires when the task with outlets succeeds – not when the DAG succeeds. Place outlets on the last meaningful data-writing task.
Cron expressions handle simple schedules. But NordGrid’s pricing pipeline runs only on trading days – weekdays, excluding Nordic public holidays. Custom timetables solve this.

blocks.definition: A Python class that replaces cron as the scheduling engine for a DAG. It implements methods that the scheduler calls to determine when the next DAG run should occur.
blocks.analogy: If cron is a wall clock that rings at fixed times, a timetable is a personal assistant who checks a calendar and only rings on working days.

blocks.content: Building a Business Day Timetable
Using the Timetable
To build reliable custom timetables, you need to understand the two methods the scheduler calls.

rows: When the next DAG run should happen
The data interval for the manual run

blocks.content: Testing Timetables
Timetables are pure Python classes. You can unit test them by calling next_dagrun_info() directly.

blocks.title: Timezone traps
blocks.content: Always use timezone-aware datetime objects in timetables. Airflow stores everything in UTC internally.
The data interval is the time range a DAG run is responsible for. Understanding and using data intervals correctly is what makes pipelines idempotent and backfillable.

blocks.title: The interval mindset
blocks.content: Every DAG run owns a slice of time. Your task code should use these boundaries to select exactly the right data.

blocks.title: Never use datetime.now()
blocks.content: If you use datetime.now() instead of data_interval_start, backfills break. Always use the injected interval variables.
Datasets and timetables are not mutually exclusive. Timetables control when a producer DAG runs. Datasets control when a consumer DAG triggers.

blocks.title: Timetables + Datasets working together
blocks.content:
PRODUCER LAYER (each has its own timetable)
  dag_bronze_meters   [schedule='@daily']
  dag_bronze_pricing  [timetable=BusinessDay]

CONSUMER LAYER (triggered by Dataset events)
  dag_silver_transform [schedule=[ALL 4 DATASETS]]
blocks.caption: Producers run on their own schedules. Consumers react to data events.

blocks.title: Loose coupling
blocks.content: The producer does not know who consumes its data. The consumer does not know the producer’s schedule. They communicate only through Dataset events.
NordGrid has 40 DAGs running on cron schedules. Converting all of them to Datasets at once would be reckless.
The Recommended Order

items: Start with leaf consumers: DAGs that consume data but produce nothing.
Move to Silver/Gold layers: Add outlets to their final write tasks.
Add outlets to Bronze producers: Keep their cron schedules.
Last: replace ExternalTaskSensor and TriggerDagRunOperator with Datasets.

blocks.title: Rollback plan
blocks.content: Keep the old cron schedule as a comment. If Dataset scheduling causes issues, reverting is a one-line change.
What You Now Know

items: Datasets are named labels that signal 'this data was updated'
Multi-Dataset scheduling uses AND logic (list) or OR logic (| operator)
Custom timetables replace cron for complex schedules using pure Python
Data intervals define the time range a DAG run owns – always use data_interval_start/end
Datasets and timetables work together: producers use timetables, consumers react to events
Migration is incremental: add outlets first, switch schedules second

blocks.content: Hands-On Exercise

items: Define five Datasets: BRONZE_METERS, BRONZE_SCADA, BRONZE_WEATHER, BRONZE_PRICING, SILVER_CLEAN.
Add outlets to each Bronze DAG’s final task.
Change the pricing DAG to use NordGridBusinessDayTimetable.
Change the Silver DAG’s schedule to require all four Bronze Datasets.
Add a quality monitoring DAG with OR scheduling.
Test the full chain end-to-end.

blocks.content: In Module 5, you will build on this Dataset-driven architecture to design multi-DAG orchestration patterns.
